section.title: Jerusalem the Unfaithful Bride
section.sectionRationale: In the Bible's longest and most searing allegory, God recounts Jerusalem's whole history as a love story turned betrayal: an abandoned infant rescued and raised, taken as a bride and adorned as a queen, who then prostitutes herself to every passing nation and idol with a promiscuity beyond even Sodom's. The allegory is deliberately shocking, using the raw language of covenant infidelity to expose sin's true nature, yet it ends where grace always ends, with God remembering His covenant and making atonement.
section.thesis: Jerusalem's entire history, found abandoned, cherished, adorned, and then unfaithful beyond any surrounding nation, is told as the story of a bride who betrays a covenant of pure grace, so that the depth of her sin has no other adequate measure than the depth of God's committed love; yet even here, grace does not merely match the sin but exceeds it, as God vows to remember His covenant and establish an everlasting one, a promise fulfilled in Christ's love for His church.
section.frameNote: Six movements: an abandoned infant found, told to live, and raised to become a covenant bride; that bride's trust betrayed as she prostitutes herself with idols made from God's own gifts; her promiscuity spreading shamelessly to surrounding nations without even the dignity of payment; the sentence of exposure and judgment pronounced; the shaming comparison to her sisters Samaria and Sodom, whom she has out-sinned; and the astonishing final turn, an everlasting covenant and atonement that outlasts every betrayal.

units.label: Found, told to live, and wed
units.note: an abandoned infant becomes a covenant bride

verses.text: Again the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, confront Jerusalem with her abominations
and tell her that this is what the Lord GOD says to Jerusalem: Your origin and your birth were in the land of the Canaanites. Your father was an Amorite and your mother a Hittite.
On the day of your birth your cord was not cut, nor were you washed with water for cleansing. You were not rubbed with salt or wrapped in cloths.
No one cared enough for you to do even one of these things out of compassion for you. Instead, you were thrown out into the open field, because you were despised on the day of your birth.
Then I passed by and saw you wallowing in your blood, and as you lay there in your blood I said to you, ‘Live!’ There I said to you, ‘Live!’
I made you thrive like a plant of the field. You grew up and matured and became very beautiful. Your breasts were formed and your hair grew, but you were naked and bare.

Then I passed by and saw you, and you were indeed old enough for love. So I spread My cloak over you and covered your nakedness. I pledged Myself to you, entered into a {{berit|covenant}} with you, and you became Mine, declares the Lord GOD.
Then I bathed you with water, rinsed off your blood, and anointed you with oil.
I clothed you in embroidered cloth and gave you sandals of fine leather. I wrapped you in fine linen and covered you with silk.
I adorned you with jewelry, and I put bracelets on your wrists and a chain around your neck.
I put a ring in your nose, earrings on your ears, and a beautiful crown upon your head.
So you were adorned with gold and silver, and your clothing was made of fine linen, silk, and embroidered cloth. You ate fine flour, honey, and oil. You became very beautiful and rose to be queen.
Your fame spread among the nations on account of your beauty, for it was perfect in the splendor I bestowed on you, declares the Lord GOD.

units.label: Trust betrayed
units.note: prostitution with idols made from God's own gifts

verses.text: But because of your fame, you trusted in your beauty and played the harlot. You lavished your favors on everyone who passed by, and your beauty was theirs for the asking.
You took some of your garments and made colorful high places for yourself, and on them you prostituted yourself. Such things should not have happened; never should they have occurred!
You also took the fine jewelry of gold and silver I had given you, and you made male idols with which to prostitute yourself.
You took your embroidered garments to cover them, and you set My oil and incense before them.
And you set before them as a pleasing aroma the food I had given you, the fine flour, oil, and honey that I had fed you. That is what happened, declares the Lord GOD.
You even took the sons and daughters you bore to Me and sacrificed them as food to idols. Was your prostitution not enough?
You slaughtered My children and delivered them up through the fire to idols.
And in all your abominations and acts of prostitution, you did not remember the days of your youth when you were naked and bare, wallowing in your own blood.

units.label: Promiscuity without shame
units.note: shrines in every street, favors given rather than sold

verses.text: Woe! Woe to you, declares the Lord GOD. And in addition to all your other wickedness,
you built yourself a mound and made yourself a lofty shrine in every public square.
At the head of every street you built your lofty shrines and degraded your beauty. With increasing promiscuity, you spread your legs to all who passed by.
You prostituted yourself with your lustful neighbors, the Egyptians, and increased your promiscuity to provoke Me to anger.
Therefore I stretched out My hand against you and reduced your portion. I gave you over to the desire of those who hate you, the daughters of the Philistines, who were ashamed of your lewd conduct.
Then you prostituted yourself with the Assyrians, because you were not yet satisfied. Even after that, you were still not satisfied.
So you extended your promiscuity to Chaldea, the land of merchants, but even with this you were not satisfied!
How weak-willed is your heart, declares the Lord GOD, while you do all these things, the acts of a shameless prostitute!
But when you built your mounds at the head of every street and made your lofty shrines in every public square, you were not even like a prostitute, because you scorned payment.
You adulterous wife! You receive strangers instead of your own husband!
Men give gifts to all their prostitutes, but you gave gifts to all your lovers. You bribed them to come to you from everywhere for your illicit favors.
So your prostitution is the opposite of that of other women: No one solicited your favors, and you paid a fee instead of receiving one; so you are the very opposite!

units.label: The sentence pronounced
units.note: exposure, judgment, and the end of wrath

verses.text: Therefore, O prostitute, hear the word of the LORD!

This is what the Lord GOD says: Because you poured out your wealth and exposed your nakedness in your promiscuity with your lovers and with all your detestable idols, and because of the blood of your children which you gave to them,

therefore I will surely gather all the lovers with whom you found pleasure, all those you loved and all those you hated. I will gather them against you from all around and expose you before them, and they will see you completely naked.
And I will sentence you to the punishment of women who commit adultery and those who shed blood; so I will bring upon you the wrath of your bloodshed and jealousy.

Then I will deliver you into the hands of your lovers, and they will level your mounds and tear down your lofty shrines. They will strip off your clothes, take your fine jewelry, and leave you naked and bare.
They will bring a mob against you, who will stone you and cut you to pieces with their swords.

Then they will burn down your houses and execute judgment against you in the sight of many women. 
I will put an end to your prostitution, and you will never again pay your lovers.
So I will lay to rest My wrath against you, and My jealousy will turn away from you. Then I will be calm and no longer angry.

Because you did not remember the days of your youth, but enraged Me with all these things, I will surely bring your deeds down upon your own head, declares the Lord GOD. Have you not committed this lewdness on top of all your other abominations?

Behold, all who speak in proverbs will quote this proverb about you: 
‘Like mother, like daughter.’

You are the daughter of your mother, who despised her husband and children. You are the sister of your sisters, who despised their husbands and children. Your mother was a Hittite and your father an Amorite.
Your older sister was Samaria, who lived with her daughters to your north; and your younger sister was Sodom, who lived with her daughters to your south.
And you not only walked in their ways and practiced their abominations, but soon you were more depraved than they were.
As surely as I live, declares the Lord GOD, your sister Sodom and her daughters never did as you and your daughters have done.
Now this was the iniquity of your sister Sodom: She and her daughters were arrogant, overfed, and complacent; they did not help the poor and needy.
Thus they were haughty and committed abominations before Me. Therefore I removed them, as you have seen.
Furthermore, Samaria did not commit half the sins you did. You have multiplied your abominations beyond theirs, and all the abominations you have committed have made your sisters appear righteous.

So now you must bear your disgrace, since you have brought justification for your sisters. For they appear more righteous than you, because your sins were more vile than theirs. So you too must bear your shame and disgrace, since you have made your sisters appear righteous.

units.label: I will remember My covenant
units.note: restoration and an everlasting covenant of atonement

verses.text: But I will restore Sodom and her daughters from captivity, as well as Samaria and her daughters. And I will restore you along with them.
So you will bear your disgrace and be ashamed of all you did to comfort them.
And your sisters, Sodom with her daughters and Samaria with her daughters, will return to their former state. You and your daughters will also return to your former state.
Did you not treat your sister Sodom as an object of scorn in the day of your pride,
before your wickedness was uncovered? Even so, you are now scorned by the daughters of Edom and all those around her, and by the daughters of the Philistines, all those around you who despise you.
You will bear the consequences of your lewdness and your abominations, declares the LORD.
For this is what the Lord GOD says: I will deal with you according to your deeds, since you have despised the oath by breaking the covenant.
But I will remember the {{berit_olam|covenant}} I made with you in the days of your youth, and I will establish an everlasting covenant with you.
Then you will remember your ways and be ashamed when you receive your older and younger sisters. I will give them to you as daughters, but not because of My covenant with you.
So I will establish My covenant with you, and you will know that I am the LORD,
so that when I make {{kipper|atonement}} for all you have done, you will remember and be ashamed and never again open your mouth because of your disgrace, declares the Lord GOD.”

berit.gloss: Covenant, a solemn, binding agreement. God's covenant with Jerusalem in verse 8 is pictured as a marriage vow, freely given to an abandoned infant with nothing to offer in return, making her later infidelity a betrayal of pure, unearned grace rather than a broken business contract.

berit_olam.gloss: Everlasting covenant. Even after cataloguing Jerusalem's covenant-breaking in exhaustive, painful detail, God promises not merely to restore the old covenant but to establish a new, everlasting one that cannot be broken again, language the New Testament applies directly to the covenant secured in Christ's blood.

kipper.gloss: To atone, to cover, to make propitiation. The chapter's final word is not merely restoration but atonement, God Himself providing the covering for sin that Jerusalem's own efforts, and even her shame, could never supply, anticipating the atonement finally accomplished at the cross.

basic.context: This is the Bible's longest and most searing allegory: Jerusalem is pictured as an abandoned infant, rescued and raised by God, taken as His bride and adorned as a queen, who then prostitutes herself with every passing nation and idol, using gifts God Himself gave her. The imagery is deliberately shocking and confronting, meant to expose exactly how serious covenant infidelity is, yet the chapter ends with God vowing to remember His covenant and provide atonement.
basic.hermeneutics: This is an extended allegory, not a literal biography, using the ancient Near Eastern language of marriage and betrayal to describe Israel's national history and covenant unfaithfulness. Read with pastoral honesty rather than either sanitizing its graphic imagery or dwelling on it gratuitously, its plain point is that idolatry is not a minor lapse but a deep betrayal of covenant love, and that grace, astonishingly, outlasts even the worst betrayal.
basic.originalLanguages: God's berit, covenant, with Jerusalem begins as an act of pure grace toward an abandoned infant with nothing to offer. The chapter's climax promises not the old covenant restored but a berit olam, an everlasting covenant, sealed by God's own kipper, atonement, providing the covering for sin that Jerusalem's shame and efforts never could.
basic.typology: Jerusalem's rescue as an abandoned infant, adorned as a bride, then unfaithful, anticipates and is answered by Christ's relationship to His church, whom He 'loved and gave Himself up for her, to sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word, that He might present the church to Himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle', Ephesians 5:25-27, the same washing, clothing, and betrothal imagery used here.
basic.christ: Where Jerusalem receives grace and betrays it, Christ is the faithful Bridegroom who loves His bride even in her unfaithfulness, cleanses her, and presents her spotless, Ephesians 5:25-27; and where this chapter ends with God remembering His covenant and making atonement despite Jerusalem's covenant-breaking, Christ is that atonement in person, His blood establishing the very everlasting covenant this chapter anticipates, Luke 22:20, Hebrews 13:20.
basic.god: God is a covenant-keeping husband whose grace toward Jerusalem begins entirely unearned and continues despite unthinkable betrayal; His jealousy and wrath are real and severe, yet they are the jealousy of a wounded love, not indifferent cruelty, and His final word is not judgment but remembered covenant and atonement.
basic.crossReferences: Hosea 1-3 tells a strikingly similar story of a faithless wife redeemed by a faithful husband. Ephesians 5:25-27 describes Christ's sanctifying, cleansing love for His bride, the church. Revelation 17-19 contrasts the great prostitute Babylon with the pure bride, the New Jerusalem, adorned for her husband. Jeremiah 31:31-34 promises the new covenant this chapter's 'everlasting covenant' anticipates.
basic.oneStory: This is Israel's whole story told as one long love story turned betrayal, an abandoned infant loved, adorned, and unfaithful beyond any surrounding nation. Yet the story does not end in exposure and shame; it ends where the whole Bible ends, with God remembering His covenant and providing atonement, a promise kept fully when Christ, the faithful Bridegroom, gives Himself for His unfaithful yet beloved bride, the church.

context.literary: The chapter is structured as an extended allegory in six movements, moving from birth and betrothal, through betrayal and shameless promiscuity, to a legal sentence, a shaming comparison with Sodom and Samaria, and finally an unexpected turn toward restoration and everlasting covenant, making it one of the most sustained single metaphors in the entire Bible.
context.historical: Addressed to a Jerusalem still standing but doomed, this allegory recounts Israel's actual historical entanglements, political and religious, with Egypt, Assyria, Philistia, and Babylon, all pursued as alliances or influences that in Ezekiel's theology amounted to spiritual prostitution against the LORD alone.
context.canonical: Draws directly on the marriage covenant imagery already used by Hosea for the northern kingdom, and becomes foundational for Ezekiel's own later allegory of Oholah and Oholibah, chapter 23, as well as for Revelation's contrast between the harlot Babylon and the bride, the New Jerusalem, Revelation 17-19, 21.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront Jerusalem with the full, unflattering truth of her covenant history, from undeserved rescue to shameless unfaithfulness, using the shock of marital betrayal imagery to make clear that her judgment is deserved beyond any comparison, while also promising, astonishingly, that God's covenant commitment will ultimately outlast even this record of betrayal.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a specific historical and theological indictment of Jerusalem's covenant unfaithfulness told through allegory; it is not a manual for marriage or a license for using explicit sexual imagery casually in teaching. Its enduring truth is the seriousness of idolatry as covenant betrayal and the depth of grace that remembers covenant even after such betrayal.

debates.question: How should preachers and teachers handle the graphic sexual imagery in this chapter responsibly?

views.label: Read it plainly but pastorally, without gratuitous elaboration
views.case: Many hold that the text's shock value is intentional and theologically necessary, meant to convey how seriously God regards idolatry, so the imagery should be acknowledged honestly rather than skipped, while being handled with restraint, care for survivors of abuse in the room, and constant attention to the chapter's ultimate message of grace.

views.label: Summarize the allegory's meaning without dwelling on graphic detail
views.case: Others, especially in mixed or vulnerable congregations, choose to summarize the narrative arc, abandonment, rescue, betrayal, judgment, restoration, without reading every graphic verse aloud, judging that the theological point can be made faithfully without extended focus on the most explicit language.

debates.bearing: Both approaches agree the imagery is deliberate and meaningful, not to be censored out of existence, and that the passage's purpose is always to expose the seriousness of sin against a backdrop of grace, never to titillate or shame vulnerable hearers.

study.originalLanguages: God's berit, covenant, with Jerusalem begins as an act of undeserved grace toward an abandoned infant with nothing to offer in return. The chapter's climax promises not merely the old covenant restored but a berit olam, an everlasting covenant, sealed by God's own kipper, atonement, providing the covering for sin that Jerusalem's own shame and effort never could supply.

typology.type: Jerusalem rescued as an abandoned infant, washed, clothed, and taken as a bride, Ezekiel 16:4-14
typology.fulfillment: Answered by Christ's love for His church, whom He cleanses by the washing of water with the word and presents to Himself in splendor, Ephesians 5:25-27.

typology.type: The everlasting covenant and atonement promised despite covenant-breaking, Ezekiel 16:60-63
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the new covenant established by Christ's blood, Luke 22:20, Hebrews 13:20, and in the atonement He accomplished once for all.

typology.type: Jerusalem the unfaithful bride contrasted with her sister cities, Ezekiel 16:44-52
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Revelation's contrast between the great prostitute Babylon and the pure bride, the New Jerusalem, adorned for her husband, Revelation 17-19, 21.

study.christ: Where Jerusalem receives undeserved grace and betrays it repeatedly and shamelessly, Christ is revealed as the faithful Bridegroom who loves His bride even in her unfaithfulness, cleanses her by the washing of water with the word, and will present her to Himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle, Ephesians 5:25-27. Where this chapter ends with God remembering His covenant and making atonement despite Jerusalem's covenant-breaking, Christ is that atonement in person, His own blood establishing the very everlasting covenant this chapter anticipates, Luke 22:20, Hebrews 13:20.
study.god: God is a covenant-keeping husband whose grace toward Jerusalem begins entirely unearned, at her point of greatest helplessness, and continues despite betrayal that this chapter insists surpasses even Sodom's; His jealousy and wrath are real and severe, the jealousy of a wounded, faithful love rather than indifferent cruelty, and His very last word in the chapter is not exposure but remembered covenant and atonement.

crossReferences.note: Tells a strikingly similar story of a faithless wife redeemed by a persistently faithful husband.
Describes Christ's sanctifying, cleansing love for His bride, the church, in language echoing this chapter's imagery.
Contrasts the great prostitute Babylon with the pure bride, the New Jerusalem, adorned for her husband.
Promises the new covenant that this chapter's 'everlasting covenant' anticipates.

study.oneStory: This is Israel's whole story told as one long love story turned betrayal, an abandoned infant loved, adorned, and unfaithful beyond any surrounding nation. Yet the story does not end in exposure and shame; it ends where the whole Bible ends, with God remembering His covenant and providing atonement, a promise kept fully when Christ, the faithful Bridegroom, gives Himself for His unfaithful yet dearly beloved bride, the church.

sermon.title: No Fidelity Without Remembering (Ezek. 16; 23)
sermon.why: Carson teaches directly and at length from Ezekiel 16, tracing Jerusalem's spiritual adultery from her rescue as an abandoned infant through her promiscuity with surrounding nations, and lands on the astonishing promise of an everlasting covenant in verses 59-63, the exact arc this study follows from grace, to betrayal, to atonement.

quiz.q: How does God describe Jerusalem's origin and earliest condition in this allegory?

options.text: An abandoned newborn, uncared for and left in the open field, whom God found and told to live.
options.note: Verses 4-6 describe this exact scene, God finding and rescuing a despised, abandoned infant.

options.text: A wealthy princess raised in comfort from birth.
options.note: The allegory begins with total abandonment and helplessness, not inherited wealth or comfort.

options.text: A city already famous for its beauty before God's involvement.
options.note: Jerusalem's later fame and beauty are explicitly credited to what God gave her, not an original condition.

options.text: A city founded by faithful worshipers of the LORD from the start.
options.note: Verse 3 explicitly traces her origin to Canaanite, Amorite, and Hittite roots, not original faithfulness.

quiz.q: What does Jerusalem do with the fine jewelry and garments God gave her?

options.text: She uses them to make idols and shrines for prostituting herself.
options.note: Verses 16-18 describe exactly this misuse of God's own gifts for idolatry.

options.text: She stores them faithfully as a memorial to God's grace.
options.note: The text describes the opposite: misuse of the gifts for idol worship.

options.text: She distributes them generously to the poor.
options.note: The gifts are turned toward idolatry, not charitable distribution, in this passage.

options.text: She sells them to fund the temple's true worship.
options.note: The gifts are explicitly repurposed for false worship, not for supporting true temple worship.

quiz.q: How does this chapter describe Jerusalem's unfaithfulness as uniquely shameless compared to an ordinary prostitute?

options.text: She paid her lovers instead of receiving payment, the opposite of a typical prostitute's arrangement.
options.note: Verse 34 states this exact reversal, that she paid a fee instead of receiving one.

options.text: She refused to have any relations with foreign nations at all.
options.note: The chapter describes extensive involvement with Egypt, Assyria, Philistia, and Chaldea, not refusal.

options.text: She only ever pursued a single foreign nation.
options.note: The text describes promiscuity with multiple nations in succession, not a single exclusive relationship.

options.text: She kept her actions entirely hidden from public view.
options.note: The chapter describes very public shrines built in every street and public square, not secrecy.

quiz.q: How does God's assessment of Jerusalem compare her to Sodom and Samaria?

options.text: Jerusalem's abominations exceeded even Sodom's, making her sisters appear more righteous by comparison.
options.note: Verses 47-52 state this explicitly and repeatedly, that Jerusalem was more depraved than either sister.

options.text: Jerusalem was found entirely innocent compared to both cities.
options.note: The passage's entire argument is that Jerusalem was worse, not innocent by comparison.

options.text: Sodom and Samaria were never actually judged for their sins.
options.note: The text explicitly notes Sodom was already removed, verse 50, for her sins.

options.text: The three cities were judged as exactly, precisely equal in guilt.
options.note: The text insists on a clear ranking, with Jerusalem's guilt exceeding both sisters', not equality.

quiz.q: What is God's ultimate promise at the close of this chapter, in verses 59-63?

options.text: He will remember the covenant made in her youth and establish an everlasting covenant, making atonement for all she has done.
options.note: Verses 60 and 63 state this promise directly, the chapter's climactic turn toward grace.

options.text: He will permanently abandon Jerusalem with no further relationship.
options.note: The chapter's final movement is toward restoration and covenant renewal, not permanent abandonment.

options.text: He will restore Jerusalem only, leaving Sodom and Samaria under judgment forever.
options.note: Verse 53 explicitly includes Sodom and Samaria in the promised restoration, not Jerusalem alone.

options.text: He will require Jerusalem to atone for her own sins before any covenant can be restored.
options.note: The text states God Himself makes the atonement, verse 63, not that Jerusalem earns or provides it herself.

questions: The chapter begins with Jerusalem utterly helpless, an abandoned infant with nothing to offer, whom God rescues purely out of grace. Where in your own life did God meet you at your point of greatest helplessness, before you had anything to offer Him?

Jerusalem used God's own gifts, her beauty, wealth, and adornment, to build idols and pursue other loves. What good gifts in your life might you be quietly redirecting toward something other than God?

This allegory is deliberately shocking to convey how seriously God regards unfaithfulness to Him. Does the severity of the imagery change how seriously you take smaller compromises or divided loyalties in your own devotion to Christ?

Despite a record of betrayal worse than Sodom's, God promises to remember His covenant and make atonement. Where do you need to believe that God's grace can reach a failure you assume is too great or too repeated to be covered?

Christ is the faithful Bridegroom who loves and cleanses His unfaithful bride rather than abandoning her. How does being loved this way, not because of your faithfulness but despite your unfaithfulness, change how you extend grace to others who have failed you?

translationNotes.note: 'I spread My cloak over you' reflects an ancient Near Eastern gesture symbolizing a marriage proposal or the assumption of protective, covenantal responsibility, similar to Ruth's request that Boaz 'spread the corner of your garment over your servant', Ruth 3:9.

'Sister' language for Samaria and Sodom does not indicate literal kinship but a shared moral category, cities compared as siblings in guilt and privilege, a common prophetic device for comparative judgment.

'The days of your youth' recalls the covenant first established at Sinai after the exodus, when Israel, like the infant of this allegory, had nothing to offer God except His own prior choice and grace toward her.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit, grouped into six thematic units given the passage's length, 63 verses, per the assignment's instruction. Sermon link verified via live web search and fetch this session, The Gospel Coalition's Carson Center podcast; this material contains graphic covenant-infidelity imagery from the source text itself and has been annotated with pastoral care rather than omitted, per the assignment's instruction to handle it honestly and never gratuitously.
